documents.t: Systems of Linear Equations Matrix Notation Elementary Row Operations The Solution Space of a System
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_01_Gaussian Elimination and Solution Spaces

documents.t: A system of mmm linear equations in nnn unknowns x1,…,xnx_1, \dots, x_nx1​,…,xn​ is expressed as: {a11x1+⋯+a1nxn=b1a21x1+⋯+a2nxn=b2⋮am1x1+⋯+amnxn=bm\begin{cases} a_{11} x_1 + \dots + a_{1n} x_n = b_1 \\ a_{21} x_1 + \dots + a_{2n} x_n = b_2 \\ \quad \vdots \\ a_{m1} x_1 + \dots + a_{mn} x_n = b_m \end{cases}⎩⎨⎧​a11​x1​+⋯+a1n​xn​=b1​a21​x1​+⋯+a2n​xn​=b2​⋮am1​x1​+⋯+amn​xn​=bm​​ Here, aija_{ij}aij​ are the coefficients and bib_ibi​ are the constants, all real numbers. A linear equation implies its solution set has an affine space structure.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_01_Gaussian Elimination and Solution Spaces

documents.t: A system of linear equations (E) can be concisely represented in matrix form as Ax=bA \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}Ax=b. AAA is the coefficient matrix: An m×nm \times nm×n matrix where A=[aij]A = [a_{ij}]A=[aij​]. x\mathbf{x}x is the matrix of unknowns/variables: An n×1n \times 1n×1 column vector containing (x1,…,xn)T(x_1, \dots, x_n)^T(x1​,…,xn​)T. b\mathbf{b}b is the matrix of constants: An m×1m \times 1m×1 column vector containing (b1,…,bm)T(b_1, \dots, b_m)^T(b1​,…,bm​)T. A system is homogeneous if b=0\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}b=0.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_01_Gaussian Elimination and Solution Spaces

documents.t: These operations are fundamental for transforming matrices, particularly in Gaussian elimination, to find solutions or simplify systems. Interchanging two rows: Ri↔RjR_i \leftrightarrow R_jRi​↔Rj​. Multiplying a row by a non-zero real number: Ri→αRiR_i \rightarrow \alpha R_iRi​→αRi​, where α∈R\alpha \in \mathbb{R}α∈R and α≠0\alpha \neq 0α=0. Adding a multiple of one row to another row: Ri→Ri+βRjR_i \rightarrow R_i + \beta R_jRi​→Ri​+βRj​, where β∈R\beta \in \mathbb{R}β∈R. These operations reveal linear dependencies among rows, which can lead to zero rows in the row-reduced form of the matrix.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_01_Gaussian Elimination and Solution Spaces

documents.t: A solution to a system (E) is an nnn-tuple α=(α1,…,αn)∈Rn\alpha = (\alpha_1, \dots, \alpha_n) \in \mathbb{R}^nα=(α1​,…,αn​)∈Rn that satisfies all equations in (E) when xi=αix_i = \alpha_ixi​=αi​. SES_ESE​: Denotes the set of all solutions to the system (E). SE0S_{E^0}SE0​: Denotes the set of all solutions to the corresponding homogeneous system E0E^0E0 (Ax=0A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}Ax=0). Theorem: If αp\alpha_pαp​ is any particular solution to a non-homogeneous system (E), then the entire solution set SES_ESE​ is given by SE=SE0+αpS_E = S_{E^0} + \alpha_pSE​=SE0​+αp​. SE0S_{E^0}SE0​ possesses a vector space structure, while SES_ESE​ (for a non-homogeneous system) is an affine space.
documents.s: The Solution Space of a System
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_01_Gaussian Elimination and Solution Spaces

documents.t: System of Linear Equations (Matrix Form): Ax=bA \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}Ax=b Elementary Row Operations: Ri↔RjR_i \leftrightarrow R_jRi​↔Rj​ Ri→αRiR_i \rightarrow \alpha R_iRi​→αRi​, α≠0\alpha \neq 0α=0 Ri→Ri+βRjR_i \rightarrow R_i + \beta R_jRi​→Ri​+βRj​ Solution Set Relationship: SE=SE0+αpS_E = S_{E^0} + \alpha_pSE​=SE0​+αp​
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_01_Gaussian Elimination and Solution Spaces

documents.t: Gaussian Elimination is a method for solving systems of linear equations, which can be represented compactly in matrix form Ax=bA \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}Ax=b. The process relies on elementary row operations to transform the matrix. The set of all solutions SES_ESE​ to a non-homogeneous system forms an affine space, directly related to the vector space of solutions SE0S_{E^0}SE0​ for its corresponding homogeneous system by adding a particular solution αp\alpha_pαp​.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_01_Gaussian Elimination and Solution Spaces

documents.t: Homogeneous Systems of Linear Equations Properties of Solutions to Homogeneous Systems Vector Subspaces Affine Spaces Properties of Vector Subspaces and Linear Equations Row-Reduced Echelon Form of a Matrix Key Formulas Quick Summary
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_02_Vector Subspaces and Row-Reduced Echelon Form

documents.t: A system of linear equations EEE has a corresponding homogeneous system E0E^0E0 where all constant terms are set to zero. For mmm equations and nnn variables, E0E^0E0 is defined as: E0:{a11x1+⋯+a1nxn=0a21x1+⋯+a2nxn=0⋮am1x1+⋯+amnxn=0E^0: \begin{cases} a_{11}x_1 + \dots + a_{1n}x_n = 0 \\ a_{21}x_1 + \dots + a_{2n}x_n = 0 \\ \vdots \\ a_{m1}x_1 + \dots + a_{mn}x_n = 0 \end{cases}E0:⎩⎨⎧​a11​x1​+⋯+a1n​xn​=0a21​x1​+⋯+a2n​xn​=0⋮am1​x1​+⋯+amn​xn​=0​ A homogeneous system always possesses the trivial solution, which is the zero vector (0,0,…,0)(0,0,\dots,0)(0,0,…,0).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_02_Vector Subspaces and Row-Reduced Echelon Form

documents.t: Let β=(β1,…,βn)\beta = (\beta_1, \dots, \beta_n)β=(β1​,…,βn​) and β′=(β1′,…,βn′)\beta' = (\beta'_1, \dots, \beta'_n)β′=(β1′​,…,βn′​) be solutions to a homogeneous system E0E^0E0. Additivity: The sum β+β′=(β1+β1′,…,βn+βn′)\beta + \beta' = (\beta_1+\beta'_1, \dots, \beta_n+\beta'_n)β+β′=(β1​+β1′​,…,βn​+βn′​) is also a solution of E0E^0E0. Homogeneity: For any scalar λ∈R\lambda \in \mathbb{R}λ∈R, the scalar multiple λβ=(λβ1,…,λβn)\lambda\beta = (\lambda\beta_1, \dots, \lambda\beta_n)λβ=(λβ1​,…,λβn​) is also a solution of E0E^0E0.
documents.s: Properties of Solutions to Homogeneous Systems
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_02_Vector Subspaces and Row-Reduced Echelon Form

documents.t: Definition: A non-empty subset SSS of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn is a vector subspace if it satisfies both the additive and homogeneous properties. Additive Property: For any u,v∈S\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in Su,v∈S, their sum u+v\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}u+v is in SSS. Homogeneous Property: For any scalar λ∈R\lambda \in \mathbb{R}λ∈R and u∈S\mathbf{u} \in Su∈S, the scalar multiple λu\lambda\mathbf{u}λu is in SSS. The set Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn denotes the Cartesian product R×⋯×R\mathbb{R} \times \dots \times \mathbb{R}R×⋯×R (nnn times), where elements are nnn-tuples of real numbers. The solution set of any homogeneous system of linear equations constitutes a vector subspace of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_02_Vector Subspaces and Row-Reduced Echelon Form

documents.t: The solution set of a non-homogeneous system of linear equations is generally not a vector subspace unless the system is homogeneous. Such solution sets are described as affine spaces, which are translates of vector subspaces by a particular solution. For example, the equation x1+x2+x3=0x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 0x1​+x2​+x3​=0 defines a plane through the origin in R3\mathbb{R}^3R3 (a vector subspace), while x1+x2+x3=1x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 1x1​+x2​+x3​=1 defines a parallel plane shifted by a vector like (1,0,0)(1,0,0)(1,0,0) (an affine space).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_02_Vector Subspaces and Row-Reduced Echelon Form

documents.t: Every vector subspace of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn must contain the origin (0,…,0)(0, \dots, 0)(0,…,0), directly stemming from the homogeneity property (0⋅v=00 \cdot \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}0⋅v=0 for any v\mathbf{v}v in the subspace). Unlike homogeneous systems, a non-homogeneous system of linear equations may have no solution. An equation is classified as linear if and only if the solution set of its homogeneous counterpart forms a vector subspace. For instance, sin⁡x=1\sin x = 1sinx=1 is not a linear equation because the solution set of sin⁡x=0\sin x = 0sinx=0 is not a vector subspace of R\mathbb{R}R.
documents.s: Properties of Vector Subspaces and Linear Equations
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_02_Vector Subspaces and Row-Reduced Echelon Form

documents.t: An r×sr \times sr×s matrix AAA can be transformed into its row-reduced echelon form (RREF) using a sequence of elementary row operations. This form is characterized by the following conditions: The first non-zero entry (called the leading entry or pivot) in each non-zero row is 111. Each leading 111 is the only non-zero entry in its respective column. All rows consisting entirely of zeros are placed at the bottom of the matrix. For any two successive non-zero rows, the leading 111 of the lower row appears to the right of the leading 111 of the upper row. Example of a matrix in RREF: (100010001)\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}​100​010​001​​
documents.s: Row-Reduced Echelon Form of a Matrix
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_02_Vector Subspaces and Row-Reduced Echelon Form

documents.t: Homogeneous System: ai1x1+⋯+ainxn=0a_{i1}x_1 + \dots + a_{in}x_n = 0ai1​x1​+⋯+ain​xn​=0 for i=1,…,mi=1, \dots, mi=1,…,m. Vector Subspace Properties: Additivity: u,v∈S ⟹ u+v∈S\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \in S \implies \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v} \in Su,v∈S⟹u+v∈S Homogeneity: λ∈R,u∈S ⟹ λu∈S\lambda \in \mathbb{R}, \mathbf{u} \in S \implies \lambda\mathbf{u} \in Sλ∈R,u∈S⟹λu∈S
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_02_Vector Subspaces and Row-Reduced Echelon Form

documents.t: This lecture covered homogeneous systems of linear equations, noting that their solutions form vector subspaces due to closure under addition and scalar multiplication. Non-homogeneous systems yield affine spaces, which are translated vector subspaces. All vector subspaces contain the origin. The row-reduced echelon form provides a unique, simplified representation of a matrix achieved through elementary row operations, characterized by leading 111s and specific zero entries.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_02_Vector Subspaces and Row-Reduced Echelon Form

documents.t: Row Echelon Form (REF) Overview Reduced Row Echelon Form (RREF) Rank of a Matrix Homogeneous Systems of Linear Equations
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_03_Reduced Row Echelon Form and Rank

documents.t: A matrix is in Row Echelon Form (REF) if it satisfies three conditions: All non-zero rows are above any rows of all zeros. The leading entry (pivot) of each non-zero row is 1. Each leading 1 is in a column to the right of the leading 1 of the row above it. REF for a given matrix is generally not unique, as different sequences of row operations can lead to different REF matrices.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_03_Reduced Row Echelon Form and Rank

documents.t: Definition: A matrix is in Reduced Row Echelon Form (RREF) if it satisfies the conditions for REF, and additionally: 4. Each column containing a leading 1 has zeros in all other positions. The RREF of any given matrix is unique.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_03_Reduced Row Echelon Form and Rank

documents.t: Definition: For an m×nm \times nm×n matrix AAA with real entries, its rank, denoted as rank(A)\text{rank}(A)rank(A), is defined as the number of non-zero rows in any row echelon form of AAA. It is a fundamental property that the row rank (number of non-zero rows in REF) equals the column rank (maximum number of linearly independent column vectors), and both are equal to the rank of the matrix.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_03_Reduced Row Echelon Form and Rank

documents.t: Consider the system of linear equations A⋅x=bA \cdot x = bA⋅x=b, where A∈Mm×n(R)A \in \mathbb{M}_{m \times n}(\mathbb{R})A∈Mm×n​(R), x∈Mn×1(R)x \in \mathbb{M}_{n \times 1}(\mathbb{R})x∈Mn×1​(R), and b∈Mm×1(R)b \in \mathbb{M}_{m \times 1}(\mathbb{R})b∈Mm×1​(R). For the homogeneous case, where b=0b = 0b=0, the system is A⋅x=0A \cdot x = 0A⋅x=0. Theorem: The homogeneous system A⋅x=0A \cdot x = 0A⋅x=0 has at least one non-zero solution in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn if and only if rank(A)\text{rank}(A) rank(A)<n nn. This theorem establishes a crucial link between the rank of the coefficient matrix and the existence of non-trivial solutions. If rank(A)\text{rank}(A) rank(A)<n nn, the columns of AAA are linearly dependent, implying a non-zero solution xxx. If A⋅x=0A \cdot x = 0A⋅x=0 has a non-zero solution, the column vectors of AAA are linearly dependent, which means rank(A)\text{rank}(A) rank(A)<n nn.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_03_Reduced Row Echelon Form and Rank

documents.t: Homogeneous linear system: A⋅x=0A \cdot x = 0A⋅x=0 Condition for non-zero solution: rank(A)\text{rank}(A) rank(A)<n nn Column vector linear combination for A⋅x=0A \cdot x = 0A⋅x=0: x1C1+x2C2+⋯+xnCn=0x_1 C_1 + x_2 C_2 + \dots + x_n C_n = 0x1​C1​+x2​C2​+⋯+xn​Cn​=0, where CiC_iCi​ are column vectors of AAA.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_03_Reduced Row Echelon Form and Rank

documents.t: Reduced Row Echelon Form (RREF) is a unique, standardized form of a matrix, characterized by leading 1s with zeros elsewhere in their columns. The rank of a matrix is the number of non-zero rows in its REF, a unique scalar value indicating the dimensionality of the vector space spanned by its rows or columns. A homogeneous system of linear equations A⋅x=0A \cdot x = 0A⋅x=0 possesses at least one non-zero solution if and only if the rank of matrix AAA is less than the number of its columns (nnn).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_03_Reduced Row Echelon Form and Rank

documents.t: Vectors in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn Linear Combinations Linear Independence and Dependence Rank and Linear Independence Standard Basis Vectors Spanning Sets Basis of a Vector Subspace Dimension of a Vector Subspace Key Formulas Quick Summary
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: A vector in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn is an nnn-tuple v=(v1,…,vn)v = (v_1, \dots, v_n)v=(v1​,…,vn​) where vi∈Rv_i \in \mathbb{R}vi​∈R. Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn is closed under vector addition and scalar multiplication, forming a vector space. The zero vector is 0=(0,0,…,0)\mathbf{0} = (0, 0, \dots, 0)0=(0,0,…,0).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: Definition: A linear combination of vectors v1,…,vmv_1, \dots, v_mv1​,…,vm​ is a vector of the form λ1v1+⋯+λmvm\lambda_1 v_1 + \dots + \lambda_m v_mλ1​v1​+⋯+λm​vm​, where λ1,…,λm\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_mλ1​,…,λm​ are real numbers (scalars).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: Definition: Vectors v1,…,vmv_1, \dots, v_mv1​,…,vm​ are linearly independent (L.I.) if the equation λ1v1+⋯+λmvm=0\lambda_1 v_1 + \dots + \lambda_m v_m = \mathbf{0}λ1​v1​+⋯+λm​vm​=0 implies that λ1=⋯=λm=0\lambda_1 = \dots = \lambda_m = 0λ1​=⋯=λm​=0. Otherwise, the vectors are linearly dependent (L.D.). If vectors are L.D., at least one scalar λi\lambda_iλi​ is non-zero, meaning one vector can be expressed as a linear combination of the others.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: Vectors v1,…,vmv_1, \dots, v_mv1​,…,vm​ are linearly independent if and only if the homogeneous system of linear equations resulting from λ1v1+⋯+λmvm=0\lambda_1 v_1 + \dots + \lambda_m v_m = \mathbf{0}λ1​v1​+⋯+λm​vm​=0 has only the trivial solution. In matrix form Aλ=0A\mathbf{\lambda} = \mathbf{0}Aλ=0 (where columns of AAA are viv_ivi​), this occurs if and only if rank(A)=m\text{rank}(A) = mrank(A)=m. There can be at most nnn linearly independent vectors in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: In Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, the standard basis vectors are e1=(1,0,…,0)e_1 = (1, 0, \dots, 0)e1​=(1,0,…,0), e2=(0,1,…,0)e_2 = (0, 1, \dots, 0)e2​=(0,1,…,0), …\dots…, en=(0,0,…,1)e_n = (0, 0, \dots, 1)en​=(0,0,…,1). These vectors are linearly independent, and any vector v=(a1,…,an)∈Rnv = (a_1, \dots, a_n) \in \mathbb{R}^nv=(a1​,…,an​)∈Rn can be uniquely written as a linear combination v=a1e1+⋯+anenv = a_1 e_1 + \dots + a_n e_nv=a1​e1​+⋯+an​en​.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: Definition: For a vector subspace WWW of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, a set of vectors {v1,…,vm}⊆W\{v_1, \dots, v_m\} \subseteq W{v1​,…,vm​}⊆W is a spanning set of WWW if every vector v∈Wv \in Wv∈W can be expressed as a linear combination of v1,…,vmv_1, \dots, v_mv1​,…,vm​. The standard basis vectors {e1,…,en}\{e_1, \dots, e_n\}{e1​,…,en​} form a spanning set for Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: Definition: A subset SSS of a vector subspace WWW is called a basis of WWW if SSS is both linearly independent and spans WWW. Theorem: Any two bases of a vector subspace of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn have the same cardinality (the same number of elements).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: Definition: The dimension of a vector subspace is the cardinality (number of elements) of any of its bases. For example, R3\mathbb{R}^3R3 has dimension 3, and a subspace defined by x+y+z=0x+y+z=0x+y+z=0 in R3\mathbb{R}^3R3 has dimension 2.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: Linear Combination: L=λ1v1+⋯+λmvmL = \lambda_1 v_1 + \dots + \lambda_m v_mL=λ1​v1​+⋯+λm​vm​ Condition for Linear Independence: λ1v1+⋯+λmvm=0 ⟹ λi=0 for all i\lambda_1 v_1 + \dots + \lambda_m v_m = \mathbf{0} \implies \lambda_i = 0 \text{ for all } iλ1​v1​+⋯+λm​vm​=0⟹λi​=0 for all i Matrix Rank for L.I.: For vectors v1,…,vmv_1, \dots, v_mv1​,…,vm​ forming columns of matrix AAA, they are L.I. iff rank(A)=m\text{rank}(A) = mrank(A)=m. Standard Basis Vectors in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn: eie_iei​ (1 at iii-th position, 0 elsewhere).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: This lecture clarified linear independence and dependence, linking them to the trivial solution of homogeneous linear systems and the rank of a matrix. Key concepts include linear combinations, the zero vector, and the standard basis of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn. A basis was defined as a linearly independent spanning set for a vector subspace, and the unique cardinality of any basis for a given subspace was introduced as its dimension.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_04_Linear Independence & Dependence

documents.t: Homogeneous Systems and Rank Existence of Solutions for Ax=bAx=bAx=b Structure of Solution Sets Linear Transformations (Operators) Key Formulas Quick Summary
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_05_Homogeneous Systems, Rank, and Linear Transformations

documents.t: Definition: System of Linear Equations A system Ax=bAx=bAx=b involves a coefficient matrix AAA, a vector of unknowns xxx, and a constant vector bbb. Definition: Homogeneous System A linear system is homogeneous if b=0b=0b=0, i.e., Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0. This system always has the zero solution (x=0x=0x=0). Theorem: Non-Zero Solutions for Homogeneous Systems A homogeneous system Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0 has a non-zero solution if and only if: rank(A)\text{rank}(A) rank(A)<n nn, where nnn is the number of unknowns (columns of AAA). The column vectors of AAA are linearly dependent. Definition: Rank of a Matrix The rank of a matrix AAA, denoted rank(A)\text{rank}(A)rank(A), is defined as: rank(A)=row-rank(A)=column-rank(A)\text{rank}(A) = \text{row-rank}(A) = \text{column-rank}(A)rank(A)=row-rank(A)=column-rank(A) The rank satisfies rank(A)≤min⁡{m,n}\text{rank}(A) \leq \min\{m, n\}rank(A)≤min{m,n}, where mmm is the number of rows and nnn is the number of columns.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_05_Homogeneous Systems, Rank, and Linear Transformations

documents.t: Theorem: Consistency Condition (Rouché-Capelli / Rank Theorem) A system of linear equations Ax=bAx=bAx=b has at least one solution if and only if the rank of the coefficient matrix AAA is equal to the rank of the augmented matrix (A∣b)(A|b)(A∣b): rank(A)=rank(A∣b)\text{rank}(A) = \text{rank}(A|b)rank(A)=rank(A∣b) The augmented matrix (A∣b)(A|b)(A∣b) is formed by appending the column vector bbb to the matrix AAA.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_05_Homogeneous Systems, Rank, and Linear Transformations

documents.t: The solution set for a consistent system Ax=bAx=bAx=b can be expressed as the sum of a particular solution xpx_pxp​ to Ax=bAx=bAx=b and the general solution S0S_0S0​ to the corresponding homogeneous system Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0: S={x∣Ax=b}={xp+xh∣Axp=b,Axh=0}S = \{x \mid Ax=b\} = \{x_p + x_h \mid Ax_p=b, Ax_h=0 \}S={x∣Ax=b}={xp​+xh​∣Axp​=b,Axh​=0} The solution set S0={x∣Ax=0}S_0 = \{x \mid Ax=0\}S0​={x∣Ax=0} is a vector subspace of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn. A basis for S0S_0S0​ consists of linearly independent vectors that span the null space of AAA, and its dimension is the nullity of AAA, given by nullity(A)=n−rank(A)\text{nullity}(A) = n - \text{rank}(A)nullity(A)=n−rank(A).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_05_Homogeneous Systems, Rank, and Linear Transformations

documents.t: Definition: Linear Transformation A function T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^mT:Rn→Rm is called a linear transformation (or linear operator) if, for all vectors x,y∈Rnx, y \in \mathbb{R}^nx,y∈Rn and all scalars λ∈R\lambda \in \mathbb{R}λ∈R, it satisfies the following two properties: Additivity: T(x+y)=T(x)+T(y)T(x+y) = T(x) + T(y)T(x+y)=T(x)+T(y) Homogeneity: T(λx)=λT(x)T(\lambda x) = \lambda T(x)T(λx)=λT(x) Properties of Linear Transformations T(0)=0T(0) = 0T(0)=0 (The zero vector in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn maps to the zero vector in Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm). T(−x)=−T(x)T(-x) = -T(x)T(−x)=−T(x). Linear transformations map lines passing through the origin in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn to lines passing through the origin in Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm. Matrix Representation of a Linear Transformation Any linear transformation T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^mT:Rn→Rm can be represented by an m×nm \times nm×n matrix AAA. If e1,e2,…,ene_1, e_2, \ldots, e_ne1​,e2​,…,en​ are the standard basis vectors for Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, then the matrix AAA has its columns given by T(e1),T(e2),…,T(en)T(e_1), T(e_2), \ldots, T(e_n)T(e1​),T(e2​),…,T(en​). Thus, T(x)=AxT(x) = AxT(x)=Ax.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_05_Homogeneous Systems, Rank, and Linear Transformations

documents.t: Homogeneous System Non-Zero Solution Condition: Ax=0 has non-zero solution ⟺ rank(A)Ax=0 \text{ has non-zero solution } \iff \text{rank}(A) Ax=0 has non-zero solution ⟺rank(A)<n nn. Consistency Condition for Ax=bAx=bAx=b: Ax=b has solution ⟺ rank(A)=rank(A∣b)Ax=b \text{ has solution } \iff \text{rank}(A) = \text{rank}(A|b)Ax=b has solution ⟺rank(A)=rank(A∣b). Structure of Solution Set: S={xp+xh∣Axp=b,Axh=0}S = \{ x_p + x_h \mid Ax_p=b, Ax_h=0 \}S={xp​+xh​∣Axp​=b,Axh​=0}, where xpx_pxp​ is a particular solution and xhx_hxh​ is a solution to the homogeneous system. Linear Transformation Properties: T(x+y)=T(x)+T(y)T(x+y) = T(x) + T(y)T(x+y)=T(x)+T(y) T(λx)=λT(x)T(\lambda x) = \lambda T(x)T(λx)=λT(x) Matrix Representation: For a linear T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^mT:Rn→Rm, T(x)=AxT(x) = AxT(x)=Ax, where A=[T(e1) T(e2) … T(en)]A = [T(e_1) \ T(e_2) \ \dots \ T(e_n)]A=[T(e1​) T(e2​) … T(en​)].
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_05_Homogeneous Systems, Rank, and Linear Transformations

documents.t: Homogeneous systems Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0 always have the zero solution; non-zero solutions exist if rank(A)\text{rank}(A)rank(A) is less than the number of unknowns (nnn), implying linearly dependent column vectors. For non-homogeneous systems Ax=bAx=bAx=b, a solution exists if and only if rank(A)=rank(A∣b)\text{rank}(A) = \text{rank}(A|b)rank(A)=rank(A∣b). The general solution to Ax=bAx=bAx=b is the sum of a particular solution and the general solution to Ax=0Ax=0Ax=0. Linear transformations T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^mT:Rn→Rm preserve vector addition and scalar multiplication, satisfy T(0)=0T(0)=0T(0)=0, and can always be represented by an m×nm \times nm×n matrix whose columns are the images of the standard basis vectors.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_05_Homogeneous Systems, Rank, and Linear Transformations

documents.t: Linear Transformations and Matrix Representation Kernel of a Linear Transformation Image of a Linear Transformation Nullity and Rank Rank-Nullity Theorem
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_06_Linear Transformations Kernel, Image, Rank-Nullity Theorem

documents.t: A function T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^mT:Rn→Rm is a linear transformation. Given the standard basis B={e1,…,en}B = \{e_1, \ldots, e_n\}B={e1​,…,en​} of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, the matrix representation of TTT with respect to BBB, denoted [T]B[T]_B[T]B​, is an m×nm \times nm×n matrix whose columns are the images of the basis vectors under TTT: [T]B=[T(e1) T(e2) … T(en)][T]_B = [T(e_1) \ T(e_2) \ \ldots \ T(e_n)][T]B​=[T(e1​) T(e2​) … T(en​)]. For any vector x∈Rnx \in \mathbb{R}^nx∈Rn, T(x)T(x)T(x) can be computed as [T]Bx[T]_B x[T]B​x.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_06_Linear Transformations Kernel, Image, Rank-Nullity Theorem

documents.t: Definition: The kernel of a linear transformation T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^mT:Rn→Rm, denoted ker(T)\text{ker}(T)ker(T), is the set of all vectors x∈Rnx \in \mathbb{R}^nx∈Rn such that T(x)=0T(x) = 0T(x)=0. The kernel is a vector subspace of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, corresponding to the solution space of the homogeneous linear system [T]Bx=0[T]_B x = 0[T]B​x=0.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_06_Linear Transformations Kernel, Image, Rank-Nullity Theorem

documents.t: Definition: The image of a linear transformation T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^mT:Rn→Rm, denoted im(T)\text{im}(T)im(T), is the set of all vectors y∈Rmy \in \mathbb{R}^my∈Rm such that y=T(x)y = T(x)y=T(x) for some x∈Rnx \in \mathbb{R}^nx∈Rn. The image is a vector subspace of Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm and is spanned by the images of the standard basis vectors, i.e., im(T)=span{T(e1),…,T(en)}\text{im}(T) = \text{span}\{T(e_1), \ldots, T(e_n)\}im(T)=span{T(e1​),…,T(en​)}.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_06_Linear Transformations Kernel, Image, Rank-Nullity Theorem

documents.t: Definition: The nullity of a linear transformation TTT, denoted nullity(T)\text{nullity}(T)nullity(T), is the dimension of its kernel: nullity(T)=dim(ker(T))\text{nullity}(T) = \text{dim}(\text{ker}(T))nullity(T)=dim(ker(T)). Definition: The rank of a linear transformation TTT, denoted rank(T)\text{rank}(T)rank(T), is the dimension of its image: rank(T)=dim(im(T))\text{rank}(T) = \text{dim}(\text{im}(T))rank(T)=dim(im(T)). The rank of TTT is equal to the rank of its matrix representation [T]B[T]_B[T]B​.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_06_Linear Transformations Kernel, Image, Rank-Nullity Theorem

documents.t: Theorem (Rank-Nullity Theorem): For a linear transformation T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^mT:Rn→Rm, the sum of its rank and nullity equals the dimension of its domain. rank(T)+nullity(T)=n\text{rank}(T) + \text{nullity}(T) = nrank(T)+nullity(T)=n
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_06_Linear Transformations Kernel, Image, Rank-Nullity Theorem

documents.t: Matrix Representation: [T]B=[T(e1) T(e2) … T(en)][T]_B = [T(e_1) \ T(e_2) \ \ldots \ T(e_n)][T]B​=[T(e1​) T(e2​) … T(en​)] Kernel Definition: ker(T)={x∈Rn∣T(x)=0}\text{ker}(T) = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid T(x) = 0\}ker(T)={x∈Rn∣T(x)=0} Image Definition: im(T)={T(x)∣x∈Rn}\text{im}(T) = \{T(x) \mid x \in \mathbb{R}^n\}im(T)={T(x)∣x∈Rn} Nullity: nullity(T)=dim(ker(T))\text{nullity}(T) = \text{dim}(\text{ker}(T))nullity(T)=dim(ker(T)) Rank: rank(T)=dim(im(T))\text{rank}(T) = \text{dim}(\text{im}(T))rank(T)=dim(im(T)) Rank-Nullity Theorem: rank(T)+nullity(T)=n\text{rank}(T) + \text{nullity}(T) = nrank(T)+nullity(T)=n
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_06_Linear Transformations Kernel, Image, Rank-Nullity Theorem

documents.t: Linear transformations T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^mT:Rn→Rm can be represented by an m×nm \times nm×n matrix [T]B[T]_B[T]B​. The kernel is the subspace of vectors mapped to zero, and its dimension is the nullity. The image is the subspace of all possible outputs, and its dimension is the rank. The Rank-Nullity Theorem states that for any linear transformation, rank(T)+nullity(T)=n\text{rank}(T) + \text{nullity}(T) = nrank(T)+nullity(T)=n, where nnn is the dimension of the domain.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_06_Linear Transformations Kernel, Image, Rank-Nullity Theorem

documents.t: For a linear transformation T:Rn→RmT: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^mT:Rn→Rm, let B1B_1B1​ be a basis for Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn and B2B_2B2​ be a basis for Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm. We can represent TTT by a matrix [T]B1B2[T]_{B_1}^{B_2}[T]B1​B2​​, whose columns are the coordinate vectors of the images of the basis vectors from B1B_1B1​ with respect to the basis B2B_2B2​.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_07

documents.t: The basis change matrix, denoted SB→CS_{B \to C}SB→C​, transforms coordinate representations of a vector from basis BBB to basis CCC. It is constructed such that its columns are the coordinate vectors of the basis vectors from BBB with respect to basis CCC. This matrix enables conversion between different coordinate systems for the same vector space.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_07

documents.t: Given a finite-dimensional vector space VVV with a basis B={b1,…,bn}B = \{b_1, \dots, b_n\}B={b1​,…,bn​}, any vector v∈Vv \in Vv∈V can be uniquely expressed as a linear combination v=α1b1+⋯+αnbnv = \alpha_1 b_1 + \dots + \alpha_n b_nv=α1​b1​+⋯+αn​bn​. The coordinate vector of vvv with respect to BBB is [v]B=(α1,…,αn)T[v]_B = (\alpha_1, \dots, \alpha_n)^T[v]B​=(α1​,…,αn​)T.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_07

documents.t: A Vector Space VVV over a field FFF is a non-empty set equipped with two operations: vector addition (+++) and scalar multiplication (⋅\cdot⋅). Vector addition makes (V,+)(V, +)(V,+) an abelian group, satisfying closure, associativity, identity (zero vector 000), inverse (for every vvv, there is −v-v−v), and commutativity. Scalar multiplication (where λ∈F,v∈V ⟹ λ⋅v∈V\lambda \in F, v \in V \implies \lambda \cdot v \in Vλ∈F,v∈V⟹λ⋅v∈V) must satisfy four additional axioms: (1) 1⋅v=v1 \cdot v = v1⋅v=v, (2) (λ+λ′)⋅v=λ⋅v+λ′⋅v(\lambda + \lambda') \cdot v = \lambda \cdot v + \lambda' \cdot v(λ+λ′)⋅v=λ⋅v+λ′⋅v, (3) λ⋅(v+w)=λ⋅v+λ⋅w\lambda \cdot (v + w) = \lambda \cdot v + \lambda \cdot wλ⋅(v+w)=λ⋅v+λ⋅w, and (4) (λμ)⋅v=λ⋅(μ⋅v)(\lambda \mu) \cdot v = \lambda \cdot (\mu \cdot v)(λμ)⋅v=λ⋅(μ⋅v).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_07

documents.t: A vector space is finite-dimensional if it has a basis consisting of a finite number of vectors; otherwise, it is infinite-dimensional. Examples include R2\mathbb{R}^2R2 and M2(R)M_2(\mathbb{R})M2​(R) (the space of 2×22 \times 22×2 matrices with real entries) as finite-dimensional vector spaces. In contrast, C[0,1]C[0,1]C[0,1] (the space of continuous functions on the interval [0,1][0,1][0,1]) is an infinite-dimensional vector space because it contains an infinite linearly independent subset, such as the set of polynomials {1,x,x2,…,xn,… }\{1, x, x^2, \dots, x^n, \dots\}{1,x,x2,…,xn,…}.
documents.s: Finite vs. Infinite Dimensional Vector Spaces
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_07

documents.t: Coordinate Transformation: [v]CT=SB→C[v]BT[v]_C^T = S_{B \to C} [v]_B^T[v]CT​=SB→C​[v]BT​ Basis Change Matrix Construction: For B={b1,…,bn}B = \{b_1, \dots, b_n\}B={b1​,…,bn​}, SB→C=([b1]CT [b2]CT … [bn]CT)S_{B \to C} = ([b_1]_C^T \ [b_2]_C^T \ \dots \ [b_n]_C^T)SB→C​=([b1​]CT​ [b2​]CT​ … [bn​]CT​) Inverse Basis Change Matrix: SC→B=(SB→C)−1S_{C \to B} = (S_{B \to C})^{-1}SC→B​=(SB→C​)−1
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_07

documents.t: Linear transformations can be represented by matrices relative to chosen bases. The basis change matrix facilitates conversion between different coordinate representations of a vector. A vector space is a fundamental algebraic structure defined by an abelian group under addition and scalar multiplication satisfying specific distributive and associative axioms. Vector spaces can be classified as finite-dimensional if they possess a finite basis or infinite-dimensional otherwise.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_07

documents.t: Linear Operators and Related Definitions Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors Characteristic Polynomial and Equation Calculating Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors Key Formulas Quick Summary
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_08_Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

documents.t: A linear operator T:V→VT: V \to VT:V→V is a linear transformation on a finite-dimensional vector space VVV over a field FFF (typically R\mathbb{R}R or C\mathbb{C}C), satisfying T(v+w)=T(v)+T(w)T(v+w)=T(v)+T(w)T(v+w)=T(v)+T(w) and T(λv)=λT(v)T(\lambda v)=\lambda T(v)T(λv)=λT(v) for all v,w∈V,λ∈Fv, w \in V, \lambda \in Fv,w∈V,λ∈F. The kernel (or null space) of TTT is Ker(T)={v∈V∣T(v)=0}\text{Ker}(T) = \{v \in V \mid T(v)=0\}Ker(T)={v∈V∣T(v)=0}, and the image (or range) of TTT is Im(T)={T(v)∣v∈V}\text{Im}(T) = \{T(v) \mid v \in V\}Im(T)={T(v)∣v∈V}. The Rank-Nullity Theorem states that for a finite-dimensional VVV, dim(Ker(T))+dim(Im(T))=dim(V)\text{dim}(\text{Ker}(T)) + \text{dim}(\text{Im}(T)) = \text{dim}(V)dim(Ker(T))+dim(Im(T))=dim(V). Examples include the derivative operator, matrix transpose, zero operator, and identity operator.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_08_Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

documents.t: An element λ∈F\lambda \in Fλ∈F is an eigenvalue of a linear operator T:V→VT: V \to VT:V→V if there exists a non-zero vector v∈Vv \in Vv∈V such that T(v)=λvT(v) = \lambda vT(v)=λv. The vector vvv is called an eigenvector corresponding to the eigenvalue λ\lambdaλ. The set of all vectors vvv satisfying T(v)=λvT(v) = \lambda vT(v)=λv (including the zero vector) forms a subspace of VVV called the eigenspace for λ\lambdaλ, denoted NλN_\lambdaNλ​. This is equivalent to finding v≠0v \neq 0v=0 such that (T−λI)v=0(T-\lambda I)v = 0(T−λI)v=0, meaning Nλ=Ker(T−λI)N_\lambda = \text{Ker}(T - \lambda I)Nλ​=Ker(T−λI).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_08_Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

documents.t: For a linear operator TTT on an nnn-dimensional vector space VVV, choosing a basis BBB allows TTT to be represented by a matrix [T]B[T]_B[T]B​. Finding eigenvalues and eigenvectors involves finding non-trivial solutions to ([T]B−λI)v=0([T]_B - \lambda I)v = 0([T]B​−λI)v=0. Such non-trivial solutions exist if and only if the matrix [T]B−λI[T]_B - \lambda I[T]B​−λI is singular, which means its rank is less than nnn. This is equivalent to det([T]B−λI)=0\text{det}([T]_B - \lambda I) = 0det([T]B​−λI)=0. The expression det([T]B−λI)\text{det}([T]_B - \lambda I)det([T]B​−λI) is a polynomial in λ\lambdaλ, known as the characteristic polynomial of TTT, denoted χT(λ)\chi_T(\lambda)χT​(λ). The equation χT(λ)=0\chi_T(\lambda) = 0χT​(λ)=0 is called the characteristic equation. The roots of the characteristic equation are the eigenvalues of TTT, and the degree of χT(λ)\chi_T(\lambda)χT​(λ) is n=dim(V)n = \text{dim}(V)n=dim(V), implying there are at most nnn eigenvalues (counted with multiplicity).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_08_Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

documents.t: To find the eigenvalues of a linear operator TTT, one constructs its matrix representation [T]B[T]_B[T]B​ with respect to a chosen basis BBB. Then, the eigenvalues λ\lambdaλ are found by solving the characteristic equation det([T]B−λI)=0\text{det}([T]_B - \lambda I) = 0det([T]B​−λI)=0. Once an eigenvalue λ\lambdaλ is determined, its corresponding eigenvectors are found by solving the homogeneous linear system ([T]B−λI)v=0([T]_B - \lambda I)v = 0([T]B​−λI)v=0 for non-zero vectors vvv. The solutions form the eigenspace NλN_\lambdaNλ​.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_08_Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

documents.t: Rank-Nullity Theorem: dim(Ker(T))+dim(Im(T))=dim(V)\text{dim}(\text{Ker}(T)) + \text{dim}(\text{Im}(T)) = \text{dim}(V)dim(Ker(T))+dim(Im(T))=dim(V) Eigenvalue/Eigenvector Definition: T(v)=λvT(v) = \lambda vT(v)=λv (for v≠0v \neq 0v=0) Eigenspace: Nλ={v∈V∣T(v)=λv}=Ker(T−λI)N_\lambda = \{v \in V \mid T(v) = \lambda v\} = \text{Ker}(T - \lambda I)Nλ​={v∈V∣T(v)=λv}=Ker(T−λI) Characteristic Equation: det([T]B−λI)=0\text{det}([T]_B - \lambda I) = 0det([T]B​−λI)=0
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_08_Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

documents.t: Linear operators map a vector space to itself. Their properties are described by the kernel (vectors mapped to zero) and image (range of the operator), linked by the Rank-Nullity Theorem. Eigenvalues are special scalars λ\lambdaλ that, when applied to a non-zero eigenvector vvv, yield the same result as the operator T(v)=λvT(v) = \lambda vT(v)=λv. These are found by solving the characteristic equation, det([T]B−λI)=0\text{det}([T]_B - \lambda I) = 0det([T]B​−λI)=0, where [T]B[T]_B[T]B​ is the matrix representation of TTT. For each eigenvalue, the corresponding eigenvectors form a subspace called the eigenspace.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_08_Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors

documents.t: Diagonalization Definition Characteristic Polynomial and Eigenvalues Geometric Multiplicity Algebraic Multiplicity Criteria for Diagonalization Example: Non-Diagonalizable Matrix Key Formulas Quick Summary
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_09_Diagonalization

documents.t: A linear operator T:V→VT: V \to VT:V→V on a finite-dimensional vector space VVV (dimension nnn) over a field FFF is diagonalizable if there exists a basis B={v1,…,vn}\mathcal{B} = \{v_1, \ldots, v_n\}B={v1​,…,vn​} of VVV such that each viv_ivi​ is an eigenvector of TTT for some eigenvalue λi∈F\lambda_i \in Fλi​∈F. This basis is called an eigenbasis. When such a basis exists, the matrix representation of TTT with respect to this eigenbasis is a diagonal matrix with entries [λ1,λ2,…,λn][\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n][λ1​,λ2​,…,λn​] along the main diagonal.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_09_Diagonalization

documents.t: For a linear operator TTT with matrix AAA (relative to some basis), its characteristic polynomial is χ(λ)=det⁡(A−λI)\chi(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)χ(λ)=det(A−λI). The roots of the characteristic equation χ(λ)=0\chi(\lambda) = 0χ(λ)=0 are the eigenvalues of TTT. A theorem states that if TTT has n=dim⁡(V)n = \dim(V)n=dim(V) distinct eigenvalues, then TTT is diagonalizable. The converse is not necessarily true; for example, the identity operator is diagonalizable but may have only one distinct eigenvalue.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_09_Diagonalization

documents.t: For an eigenvalue λ∈F\lambda \in Fλ∈F, the eigenspace Nλ=ker⁡(T−λI)={v∈V∣T(v)=λv}N_\lambda = \ker(T - \lambda I) = \{v \in V \mid T(v) = \lambda v\}Nλ​=ker(T−λI)={v∈V∣T(v)=λv} is a subspace of VVV comprising all eigenvectors corresponding to λ\lambdaλ (and the zero vector). The geometric multiplicity of λ\lambdaλ, denoted gλg_\lambdagλ​, is defined as the dimension of its eigenspace: gλ=dim⁡(Nλ)g_\lambda = \dim(N_\lambda)gλ​=dim(Nλ​). It signifies the maximum number of linearly independent eigenvectors associated with λ\lambdaλ.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_09_Diagonalization

documents.t: Assuming the characteristic polynomial χ(λ)\chi(\lambda)χ(λ) has all its roots in the field FFF, it can be factored as χ(λ)=(λ−λ1)e1⋯(λ−λr)er\chi(\lambda) = (\lambda - \lambda_1)^{e_1} \cdots (\lambda - \lambda_r)^{e_r}χ(λ)=(λ−λ1​)e1​⋯(λ−λr​)er​, where λ1,…,λr\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_rλ1​,…,λr​ are the distinct eigenvalues. The exponent ej≥1e_j \geq 1ej​≥1 is called the algebraic multiplicity of the eigenvalue λj\lambda_jλj​. The sum of all algebraic multiplicities equals the dimension of the vector space: ∑j=1rej=n=dim⁡(V)\sum_{j=1}^r e_j = n = \dim(V)∑j=1r​ej​=n=dim(V).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_09_Diagonalization

documents.t: For any eigenvalue λi\lambda_iλi​, its geometric multiplicity is always less than or equal to its algebraic multiplicity: gi≤eig_i \leq e_igi​≤ei​. A linear operator TTT (or its matrix AAA) is diagonalizable if and only if the geometric multiplicity equals the algebraic multiplicity for every eigenvalue: gi=eig_i = e_igi​=ei​ for all i=1,…,ri = 1, \ldots, ri=1,…,r.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_09_Diagonalization

documents.t: Consider the matrix A=(1−10010012)A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}A=​100​−111​002​​. The characteristic polynomial is χ(λ)=det⁡(A−λI)=(1−λ)2(2−λ)=0\chi(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I) = (1-\lambda)^2(2-\lambda) = 0χ(λ)=det(A−λI)=(1−λ)2(2−λ)=0. The eigenvalues are λ1=1\lambda_1 = 1λ1​=1 with algebraic multiplicity e1=2e_1 = 2e1​=2, and λ2=2\lambda_2 = 2λ2​=2 with algebraic multiplicity e2=1e_2 = 1e2​=1. For λ2=2\lambda_2 = 2λ2​=2, its geometric multiplicity g2=1g_2 = 1g2​=1, which equals its algebraic multiplicity e2e_2e2​. For λ1=1\lambda_1 = 1λ1​=1, we find the eigenspace N1=ker⁡(A−I)N_1 = \ker(A - I)N1​=ker(A−I). Solving (A−I)v=0(A-I)v=0(A−I)v=0 for v=(v1,v2,v3)v=(v_1, v_2, v_3)v=(v1​,v2​,v3​) yields v2=0v_2=0v2​=0 and v3=−v1v_3=-v_1v3​=−v1​. Thus, N1={(t,0,−t)∣t∈R}N_1 = \{(t, 0, -t) \mid t \in \mathbb{R}\}N1​={(t,0,−t)∣t∈R}, and its dimension, the geometric multiplicity g1=dim⁡(N1)=1g_1 = \dim(N_1) = 1g1​=dim(N1​)=1. Since g1=1g_1 = 1 g1​=1<e1=2 e_1 = 2e1​=2, the matrix AAA is not diagonalizable. (Exercise: Prove that eigenvectors corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are linearly independent.)
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_09_Diagonalization

documents.t: Characteristic Polynomial: χ(λ)=det⁡(A−λI)\chi(\lambda) = \det(A - \lambda I)χ(λ)=det(A−λI) Eigenspace for λ\lambdaλ: Nλ=ker⁡(T−λI)={v∈V∣T(v)=λv}N_\lambda = \ker(T - \lambda I) = \{v \in V \mid T(v) = \lambda v\}Nλ​=ker(T−λI)={v∈V∣T(v)=λv} Geometric Multiplicity: gλ=dim⁡(Nλ)g_\lambda = \dim(N_\lambda)gλ​=dim(Nλ​) Algebraic Multiplicity: eλe_\lambdaeλ​, the exponent of (λ−λi)(\lambda - \lambda_i)(λ−λi​) in χ(λ)\chi(\lambda)χ(λ) Diagonalization Condition: TTT is diagonalizable ⟺ gλ=eλ\iff g_\lambda = e_\lambda⟺gλ​=eλ​ for all eigenvalues λ\lambdaλ.

documents.t: Diagonalization allows representing a linear operator using a simple diagonal matrix when an eigenbasis exists. This is possible if and only if the geometric multiplicity of each eigenvalue equals its algebraic multiplicity. While having nnn distinct eigenvalues for an nnn-dimensional space guarantees diagonalizability, it is not a necessary condition. The eigenspace's dimension gives the geometric multiplicity, while the exponent in the characteristic polynomial gives the algebraic multiplicity.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_09_Diagonalization

documents.t: An Inner Product Space (V,⟨⋅,⋅⟩)(V, \langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle)(V,⟨⋅,⋅⟩) is a vector space VVV over a field F\mathbb{F}F (R\mathbb{R}R or C\mathbb{C}C) equipped with an inner product, a function ⟨⋅,⋅⟩:V×V→F\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle: V \times V \to \mathbb{F}⟨⋅,⋅⟩:V×V→F that satisfies the following axioms for all x,y,z∈Vx, y, z \in Vx,y,z∈V and c∈Fc \in \mathbb{F}c∈F: Additivity in the first argument: ⟨x+y,z⟩=⟨x,z⟩+⟨y,z⟩\langle x+y, z \rangle = \langle x,z \rangle + \langle y,z \rangle⟨x+y,z⟩=⟨x,z⟩+⟨y,z⟩. Homogeneity in the first argument: ⟨cx,y⟩=c⟨x,y⟩\langle cx, y \rangle = c \langle x,y \rangle⟨cx,y⟩=c⟨x,y⟩. Conjugate symmetry: ⟨x,y⟩=⟨y,x⟩‾\langle x,y \rangle = \overline{\langle y,x \rangle}⟨x,y⟩=⟨y,x⟩​. (For F=R\mathbb{F}=\mathbb{R}F=R, this simplifies to ⟨x,y⟩=⟨y,x⟩\langle x,y \rangle = \langle y,x \rangle⟨x,y⟩=⟨y,x⟩.) Positive-definiteness: ⟨x,x⟩≥0\langle x,x \rangle \ge 0⟨x,x⟩≥0, and ⟨x,x⟩=0\langle x,x \rangle = 0⟨x,x⟩=0 if and only if x=0x=0x=0. Common examples include the usual dot product in Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn, the complex dot product in Cn\mathbb{C}^nCn, the integral of products for continuous functions C[0,1]C[0,1]C[0,1], and tr(B∗A)\text{tr}(B^*A)tr(B∗A) for matrices Mn(F)M_n(\mathbb{F})Mn​(F).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_10_Inner Product Spaces

documents.t: Theorem: If VVV is an Inner Product Space over F\mathbb{F}F, then for all x,y,z∈Vx, y, z \in Vx,y,z∈V and c∈Fc \in \mathbb{F}c∈F, the following properties hold: ⟨x,y+z⟩=⟨x,y⟩+⟨x,z⟩\langle x, y+z \rangle = \langle x,y \rangle + \langle x,z \rangle⟨x,y+z⟩=⟨x,y⟩+⟨x,z⟩ ⟨x,cy⟩=cˉ⟨x,y⟩\langle x, cy \rangle = \bar{c} \langle x,y \rangle⟨x,cy⟩=cˉ⟨x,y⟩ ⟨x,0⟩=⟨0,x⟩=0\langle x, 0 \rangle = \langle 0, x \rangle = 0⟨x,0⟩=⟨0,x⟩=0 If ⟨x,y⟩=⟨x,z⟩\langle x,y \rangle = \langle x,z \rangle⟨x,y⟩=⟨x,z⟩ for all x∈Vx \in Vx∈V, then y=zy=zy=z.

documents.t: The norm (or length) of a vector x∈Vx \in Vx∈V in an Inner Product Space is defined as ∣∣x∣∣=⟨x,x⟩||x|| = \sqrt{\langle x,x \rangle}∣∣x∣∣=⟨x,x⟩​. From this definition, the following properties are derived: ∣∣cx∣∣=∣c∣∣∣x∣∣||cx|| = |c| ||x||∣∣cx∣∣=∣c∣∣∣x∣∣ for any scalar c∈Fc \in \mathbb{F}c∈F. ∣∣x∣∣=0||x|| = 0∣∣x∣∣=0 if and only if x=0x=0x=0. (Note that ∣∣x∣∣||x|| ∣∣x∣∣>0 00 if x≠0x \ne 0x=0).
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_10_Inner Product Spaces

documents.t: Theorem (Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality): For any vectors x,yx, yx,y in an Inner Product Space VVV, the absolute value of their inner product is less than or equal to the product of their norms: ∣⟨x,y⟩∣≤∣∣x∣∣⋅∣∣y∣∣|\langle x,y \rangle| \le ||x|| \cdot ||y||∣⟨x,y⟩∣≤∣∣x∣∣⋅∣∣y∣∣
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_10_Inner Product Spaces

documents.t: Theorem (Triangle Inequality): For any vectors x,yx, yx,y in an Inner Product Space VVV, the norm of their sum is less than or equal to the sum of their norms: ∣∣x+y∣∣≤∣∣x∣∣+∣∣y∣∣||x+y|| \le ||x|| + ||y||∣∣x+y∣∣≤∣∣x∣∣+∣∣y∣∣
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_10_Inner Product Spaces

documents.t: Orthogonal Vectors: Two vectors x,y∈Vx, y \in Vx,y∈V are orthogonal if their inner product is zero: ⟨x,y⟩=0\langle x,y \rangle = 0⟨x,y⟩=0. Orthogonal Set: A set S⊆VS \subseteq VS⊆V is an orthogonal set of vectors if every distinct pair of vectors in SSS is orthogonal: ⟨x,y⟩=0\langle x,y \rangle = 0⟨x,y⟩=0 for all x,y∈Sx,y \in Sx,y∈S with x≠yx \ne yx=y. Orthonormal Set: An orthonormal set S⊆VS \subseteq VS⊆V is an orthogonal set where every vector in SSS has a norm of 1: ∣∣x∣∣=1||x||=1∣∣x∣∣=1 for all x∈Sx \in Sx∈S.

documents.t: A norm can also be independently defined as a function ∣∣⋅∣∣:V→F||\cdot||: V \to \mathbb{F}∣∣⋅∣∣:V→F (where F\mathbb{F}F is R\mathbb{R}R or C\mathbb{C}C) satisfying the following axioms for all x,y∈Vx, y \in Vx,y∈V and c∈Fc \in \mathbb{F}c∈F: Positive-definiteness: ∣∣x∣∣≥0||x|| \ge 0∣∣x∣∣≥0, and ∣∣x∣∣=0||x|| = 0∣∣x∣∣=0 if and only if x=0x=0x=0. Absolute homogeneity: ∣∣cx∣∣=∣c∣∣∣x∣∣||cx|| = |c| ||x||∣∣cx∣∣=∣c∣∣∣x∣∣. Triangle inequality: ∣∣x+y∣∣≤∣∣x∣∣+∣∣y∣∣||x+y|| \le ||x|| + ||y||∣∣x+y∣∣≤∣∣x∣∣+∣∣y∣∣.

documents.t: Inner Product Axioms: ⟨x+y,z⟩=⟨x,z⟩+⟨y,z⟩\langle x+y, z \rangle = \langle x,z \rangle + \langle y,z \rangle⟨x+y,z⟩=⟨x,z⟩+⟨y,z⟩ ⟨cx,y⟩=c⟨x,y⟩\langle cx, y \rangle = c \langle x,y \rangle⟨cx,y⟩=c⟨x,y⟩ ⟨x,y⟩=⟨y,x⟩‾\langle x,y \rangle = \overline{\langle y,x \rangle}⟨x,y⟩=⟨y,x⟩​ ⟨x,x⟩≥0\langle x,x \rangle \ge 0⟨x,x⟩≥0 and ⟨x,x⟩=0 ⟺ x=0\langle x,x \rangle = 0 \iff x=0⟨x,x⟩=0⟺x=0 Derived Inner Product Properties: ⟨x,y+z⟩=⟨x,y⟩+⟨x,z⟩\langle x, y+z \rangle = \langle x,y \rangle + \langle x,z \rangle⟨x,y+z⟩=⟨x,y⟩+⟨x,z⟩ ⟨x,cy⟩=cˉ⟨x,y⟩\langle x, cy \rangle = \bar{c} \langle x,y \rangle⟨x,cy⟩=cˉ⟨x,y⟩ ⟨x,0⟩=⟨0,x⟩=0\langle x, 0 \rangle = \langle 0, x \rangle = 0⟨x,0⟩=⟨0,x⟩=0 Norm Definition: ∣∣x∣∣=⟨x,x⟩||x|| = \sqrt{\langle x,x \rangle}∣∣x∣∣=⟨x,x⟩​ Derived Norm Properties: ∣∣cx∣∣=∣c∣∣∣x∣∣||cx|| = |c| ||x||∣∣cx∣∣=∣c∣∣∣x∣∣ ∣∣x∣∣=0 ⟺ x=0||x|| = 0 \iff x=0∣∣x∣∣=0⟺x=0 Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality: ∣⟨x,y⟩∣≤∣∣x∣∣⋅∣∣y∣∣|\langle x,y \rangle| \le ||x|| \cdot ||y||∣⟨x,y⟩∣≤∣∣x∣∣⋅∣∣y∣∣ Triangle Inequality: ∣∣x+y∣∣≤∣∣x∣∣+∣∣y∣∣||x+y|| \le ||x|| + ||y||∣∣x+y∣∣≤∣∣x∣∣+∣∣y∣∣ Orthogonality Condition: ⟨x,y⟩=0\langle x,y \rangle = 0⟨x,y⟩=0 Independent Norm Axioms: ∣∣x∣∣≥0||x|| \ge 0∣∣x∣∣≥0 and ∣∣x∣∣=0 ⟺ x=0||x|| = 0 \iff x=0∣∣x∣∣=0⟺x=0 ∣∣cx∣∣=∣c∣∣∣x∣∣||cx|| = |c| ||x||∣∣cx∣∣=∣c∣∣∣x∣∣ ∣∣x+y∣∣≤∣∣x∣∣+∣∣y∣∣||x+y|| \le ||x|| + ||y||∣∣x+y∣∣≤∣∣x∣∣+∣∣y∣∣
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_10_Inner Product Spaces

documents.t: Inner Product Spaces extend the concept of dot product, allowing for notions of length (norm) and angle (orthogonality) in abstract vector spaces. An inner product satisfies linearity, conjugate symmetry, and positive-definiteness. The norm is derived from the inner product, giving rise to fundamental relationships like the Cauchy-Schwarz and Triangle Inequalities. Vectors are orthogonal if their inner product is zero, and an orthonormal set consists of orthogonal vectors with unit norm. A norm can also be defined independently, adhering to positive-definiteness, absolute homogeneity, and the triangle inequality.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Linear Algebra/Lec_10_Inner Product Spaces

documents.t: The Gram-Schmidt (G-S) process is a method to obtain an orthonormal basis from any basis of a finite-dimensional Inner Product Space (IPS). Theorem (Orthogonal Set Basis): If VVV is an IPS and S={v1,…,vk}S = \{v_1, \dots, v_k\}S={v1​,…,vk​} is an orthogonal set with vi≠0v_i \neq 0vi​=0 for all iii, then for any y∈Span(S)y \in \text{Span}(S)y∈Span(S), y=∑i=1k⟨y,vi⟩∥vi∥2viy = \sum_{i=1}^k \frac{\langle y, v_i \rangle}{\|v_i\|^2} v_iy=∑i=1k​∥vi​∥2⟨y,vi​⟩​vi​ If SSS is orthonormal, then y=∑i=1k⟨y,vi⟩viy = \sum_{i=1}^k \langle y, v_i \rangle v_iy=∑i=1k​⟨y,vi​⟩vi​. An orthogonal set of non-zero vectors is linearly independent. Theorem (Gram-Schmidt Process): Let VVV be an IPS, and S={w1,…,wn}S = \{w_1, \dots, w_n\}S={w1​,…,wn​} be a linearly independent set of VVV. Define S′={v1,…,vn}S' = \{v_1, \dots, v_n\}S′={v1​,…,vn​} as: v1=w1v_1 = w_1v1​=w1​ vk=wk−∑j=1k−1⟨wk,vj⟩∥vj∥2vjfor 2≤k≤nv_k = w_k - \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \frac{\langle w_k, v_j \rangle}{\|v_j\|^2} v_j \quad \text{for } 2 \le k \le nvk​=wk​−∑j=1k−1​∥vj​∥2⟨wk​,vj​⟩​vj​for 2≤k≤n Then S′S'S′ is an orthogonal set of nonzero vectors, and Span(S′)=Span(S)\text{Span}(S') = \text{Span}(S)Span(S′)=Span(S). If SSS is a basis for VVV, the G-S method yields an orthonormal basis S′S'S′ for VVV by normalizing the vkv_kvk​ vectors.

documents.t: Definition: For a subset SSS of an IPS VVV, its orthogonal complement S⊥S^\perpS⊥ is defined as: S⊥={x∈V∣⟨x,y⟩=0 for all y∈S}S^\perp = \{x \in V \mid \langle x, y \rangle = 0 \text{ for all } y \in S\}S⊥={x∈V∣⟨x,y⟩=0 for all y∈S} Theorem (Orthogonal Decomposition): Let WWW be a finite-dimensional subspace of an IPS VVV. For any y∈Vy \in Vy∈V, there exist unique vectors u∈Wu \in Wu∈W and z∈W⊥z \in W^\perpz∈W⊥ such that y=u+zy = u + zy=u+z. Furthermore, if {v1,…,vk}\{v_1, \dots, v_k\}{v1​,…,vk​} is an orthonormal basis (ONB) of WWW, then u=∑i=1k⟨y,vi⟩viu = \sum_{i=1}^k \langle y, v_i \rangle v_iu=∑i=1k​⟨y,vi​⟩vi​ The vector uuu is the unique vector in WWW that is closest to yyy, meaning ∥y−x∥≥∥y−u∥\|y - x\| \ge \|y - u\|∥y−x∥≥∥y−u∥ for all x∈Wx \in Wx∈W, and equality holds if and only if x=ux=ux=u.

documents.t: Given a data set {(t1,y1),…,(tm,ym)}\{(t_1, y_1), \dots, (t_m, y_m)\}{(t1​,y1​),…,(tm​,ym​)}, where yiy_iyi​ is the observed value at time tit_iti​. If there is an essential linear relationship (y=ct+dy = ct + dy=ct+d), the goal is to find ccc and ddd such that the error E=∑i=1m(yi−cti−d)2E = \sum_{i=1}^m (y_i - ct_i - d)^2E=∑i=1m​(yi​−cti​−d)2 is minimized. This line is called the least squares line. This problem can be formulated in matrix form: Let A=(t11⋮⋮tm1),x=(cd),y=(y1⋮ym)A = \begin{pmatrix} t_1 & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ t_m & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad x = \begin{pmatrix} c \\ d \end{pmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{pmatrix} y_1 \\ \vdots \\ y_m \end{pmatrix}A=​t1​⋮tm​​1⋮1​​,x=(cd​),y=​y1​⋮ym​​​ Then E=∥y−Ax∥2E = \|y - Ax\|^2E=∥y−Ax∥2. The problem is to find x0∈Fnx_0 \in \mathbb{F}^nx0​∈Fn such that EEE is minimized, i.e., ∥y−Ax0∥≤∥y−Ax∥\|y - Ax_0\| \le \|y - Ax\|∥y−Ax0​∥≤∥y−Ax∥ for all x∈Fnx \in \mathbb{F}^nx∈Fn.

documents.t: Definition: For an m×nm \times nm×n matrix A∈IMm,n(F)A \in \text{IM}_{m,n}(\mathbb{F})A∈IMm,n​(F), its adjoint A∗A^*A∗ is defined as A∗=(Aji‾)t=(A‾)tA^* = (\overline{A_{ji}})^t = (\overline{A})^tA∗=(Aji​​)t=(A)t. For a linear operator T:V→VT: V \to VT:V→V in an IPS VVV, its adjoint T∗:V→VT^*: V \to VT∗:V→V is a unique linear operator such that ⟨T(x),y⟩=⟨x,T∗(y)⟩\langle T(x), y \rangle = \langle x, T^*(y) \rangle⟨T(x),y⟩=⟨x,T∗(y)⟩ for all x,y∈Vx, y \in Vx,y∈V. The matrix representation of T∗T^*T∗ with respect to an orthonormal basis B\mathcal{B}B is [T∗]B=([T]B)∗[T^*]_\mathcal{B} = ([T]_\mathcal{B})^*[T∗]B​=([T]B​)∗. Theorem (Least Squares Solution): Let A∈IMm,n(F)A \in \text{IM}_{m,n}(\mathbb{F})A∈IMm,n​(F) and y∈Fmy \in \mathbb{F}^my∈Fm. There exists x0∈Fnx_0 \in \mathbb{F}^nx0​∈Fn such that (A∗A)x0=A∗y(A^*A)x_0 = A^*y(A∗A)x0​=A∗y (Normal Equations), and ∥Ax0−y∥≤∥Ax−y∥\|Ax_0 - y\| \le \|Ax - y\|∥Ax0​−y∥≤∥Ax−y∥ for all x∈Fnx \in \mathbb{F}^nx∈Fn. Furthermore, if rank(A)=n\text{rank}(A) = nrank(A)=n, then A∗AA^*AA∗A is invertible, and the unique solution is x0=(A∗A)−1A∗yx_0 = (A^*A)^{-1}A^*yx0​=(A∗A)−1A∗y. This relies on the fact that rank(A∗A)=rank(A)\text{rank}(A^*A) = \text{rank}(A)rank(A∗A)=rank(A).

Least squares error: E=∥y−Ax∥2E = \|y - Ax\|^2E=∥y−Ax∥2 Normal equations: (A∗A)x0=A∗y(A^*A)x_0 = A^*y(A∗A)x0​=A∗y Least squares solution (if rank(A)=n): x0=(A∗A)−1A∗yx_0 = (A^*A)^{-1}A^*yx0​=(A∗A)−1A∗y Adjoint operator property: ⟨T(x),y⟩=⟨x,T∗(y)⟩\langle T(x), y \rangle = \langle x, T^*(y) \rangle⟨T(x),y⟩=⟨x,T∗(y)⟩

documents.t: This lecture covered crucial methods for working with Inner Product Spaces and linear data fitting. The Gram-Schmidt process provides a systematic way to convert any basis into an orthonormal one. We defined the orthogonal complement and explored the unique decomposition of vectors into components within a subspace and its complement, with the closest vector property. Finally, we introduced the least squares approximation method to find the best linear fit for data, derived its solution using the normal equations, and defined the adjoint operator in both matrix and abstract linear operator contexts.

documents.t: The instantaneous rate of change represents how fast a quantity is changing at a specific moment. It is approached by examining average rates of change over increasingly smaller intervals, leading to the concept of limits and the slope of a tangent line.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Single Variable Calculus/Lec_01_Limit and Continuity

documents.t: Definition: Suppose a function f(x)f(x)f(x) is defined for all xxx near a point aaa, except possibly at aaa itself. If f(x)f(x)f(x) gets arbitrarily close to a number LLL as xxx gets sufficiently close to aaa (but not equal to aaa), we say that fff approaches the limit LLL as xxx approaches aaa, and write: lim⁡x→af(x)=L\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = Llimx→a​f(x)=L The existence of a limit at aaa does not depend on the function's value at aaa.

documents.t: Definition: Suppose fff is defined for all xxx near aaa. If f(x)f(x)f(x) grows arbitrarily large for all xxx sufficiently close (but not equal) to aaa, we write: lim⁡x→af(x)=∞\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = \inftylimx→a​f(x)=∞ If f(x)f(x)f(x) is negative and grows arbitrarily large in magnitude for all xxx sufficiently close (but not equal) to aaa, we write: lim⁡x→af(x)=−∞\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = -\inftylimx→a​f(x)=−∞ In both cases, the limit does not exist.

documents.t: Theorem: Assume fff is defined for all xxx near aaa except possibly at aaa. Then, the two-sided limit lim⁡x→af(x)=L\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = Llimx→a​f(x)=L if and only if both the right-sided limit and the left-sided limit exist and are equal to LLL: lim⁡x→af(x)=Lifflim⁡x→a+f(x)=Landlim⁡x→a−f(x)=L\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = L \quad \text{iff} \quad \lim_{x \to a^+} f(x) = L \quad \text{and} \quad \lim_{x \to a^-} f(x) = Llimx→a​f(x)=Lifflimx→a+​f(x)=Landlimx→a−​f(x)=L If one-sided limits differ or do not exist, the two-sided limit does not exist.

documents.t: Theorem 1: If L,M,c,L, M, c,L,M,c, and kkk are real numbers, and lim⁡x→cf(x)=L\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = Llimx→c​f(x)=L and lim⁡x→cg(x)=M\lim_{x \to c} g(x) = Mlimx→c​g(x)=M, then: Sum Rule: lim⁡x→c(f(x)+g(x))=L+M\lim_{x \to c}(f(x) + g(x)) = L + Mlimx→c​(f(x)+g(x))=L+M Difference Rule: lim⁡x→c(f(x)−g(x))=L−M\lim_{x \to c}(f(x) - g(x)) = L - Mlimx→c​(f(x)−g(x))=L−M Constant Multiple Rule: lim⁡x→c(k⋅f(x))=k⋅L\lim_{x \to c}(k \cdot f(x)) = k \cdot Llimx→c​(k⋅f(x))=k⋅L Product Rule: lim⁡x→c(f(x)⋅g(x))=L⋅M\lim_{x \to c}(f(x) \cdot g(x)) = L \cdot Mlimx→c​(f(x)⋅g(x))=L⋅M Quotient Rule: lim⁡x→cf(x)g(x)=LM\lim_{x \to c}\frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \frac{L}{M}limx→c​g(x)f(x)​=ML​, provided M≠0M \neq 0M=0 Power Rule: lim⁡x→c[f(x)]n=Ln\lim_{x \to c}[f(x)]^n = L^nlimx→c​[f(x)]n=Ln, for a positive integer nnn Root Rule: lim⁡x→cf(x)n=Ln=L1/n\lim_{x \to c}\sqrt[n]{f(x)} = \sqrt[n]{L} = L^{1/n}limx→c​nf(x)​=nL​=L1/n, for a positive integer nnn. documents.t: Definition: Let f(x)f(x)f(x) be defined on an open interval about ccc, except possibly at ccc itself. We say that the limit of f(x)f(x)f(x) as xxx approaches ccc is the number LLL, and write lim⁡x→cf(x)=L\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = Llimx→c​f(x)=L, if for every number ϵ\epsilon ϵ>0 00, there exists a corresponding number δ\delta δ>0 00 such that for all xxx: 0 $<$ |x - c| $<$ \delta \implies |f(x) - L| $<$ \epsilon This means that f(x)f(x)f(x) can be made arbitrarily close to LLL (within ϵ\epsilonϵ) by taking xxx sufficiently close to ccc (within δ\deltaδ), but not equal to ccc.

documents.t: Let ccc be a real number on the xxx-axis. Continuous at ccc: The function fff is continuous at ccc if: f(c)f(c)f(c) is defined. lim⁡x→cf(x)\lim_{x \to c} f(x)limx→c​f(x) exists. lim⁡x→cf(x)=f(c)\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = f(c)limx→c​f(x)=f(c). Right-continuous at ccc: The function fff is right-continuous at ccc if: lim⁡x→c+f(x)=f(c)\lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) = f(c)limx→c+​f(x)=f(c) Left-continuous at ccc: The function fff is left-continuous at ccc if: lim⁡x→c−f(x)=f(c)\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) = f(c)limx→c−​f(x)=f(c)

documents.t: Removable Discontinuity: Occurs when lim⁡x→cf(x)\lim_{x \to c} f(x)limx→c​f(x) exists, but f(c)f(c)f(c) is either undefined or f(c)≠lim⁡x→cf(x)f(c) \neq \lim_{x \to c} f(x)f(c)=limx→c​f(x). This can often be "removed" by redefining f(c)f(c)f(c). Jump Discontinuity: Occurs when the one-sided limits exist but are not equal, i.e., lim⁡x→c−f(x)≠lim⁡x→c+f(x)\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) \neq \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x)limx→c−​f(x)=limx→c+​f(x). Example: the greatest integer function at integer values. Infinite Discontinuity: Occurs when lim⁡x→cf(x)=±∞\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = \pm \inftylimx→c​f(x)=±∞. This is characterized by a vertical asymptote at x=cx=cx=c. Example: f(x)=1/x2f(x) = 1/x^2f(x)=1/x2 at x=0x=0x=0. Oscillating Discontinuity: Occurs when the function values oscillate infinitely often as xxx approaches ccc, preventing any limit (even one-sided) from existing. Example: f(x)=sin⁡(1/x)f(x) = \sin(1/x)f(x)=sin(1/x) at x=0x=0x=0.

Special Limit: lim⁡θ→0sin⁡θθ=1\lim_{\theta \to 0} \frac{\sin \theta}{\theta} = 1limθ→0​θsinθ​=1 Epsilon-Delta Limit Definition: 00 0<∣x−c∣|x - c|∣x−c∣<δ ⟹ ∣f(x)−L∣\delta \implies |f(x) - L|δ⟹∣f(x)−L∣<ϵ \epsilonϵ Continuity at ccc: lim⁡x→cf(x)=f(c)\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = f(c)limx→c​f(x)=f(c)

documents.t: This lecture established limits as a fundamental concept for understanding instantaneous rates of change and tangent lines. We defined limits, including one-sided and infinite limits, and explored their properties through limit laws and the Sandwich Theorem. The formal epsilon-delta definition provided a rigorous basis. Finally, continuity was defined based on limits, and various types of discontinuities were categorized.

documents.t: Theorem: Let fff be a continuous function on a closed interval [a,b][a,b][a,b]. Then fff is bounded on [a,b][a,b][a,b]. That is, there is a number CC C>0 00 such that ∣f(x)∣≤C|f(x)| \leq C∣f(x)∣≤C for all x∈[a,b]x \in [a,b]x∈[a,b].

documents.t: Average Rate of Change: For a function fff on [a,a+h][a, a+h][a,a+h], msec=f(a+h)−f(a)hm_{sec} = \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}msec​=hf(a+h)−f(a)​. Instantaneous Rate of Change (Derivative): The slope of the tangent line at (a,f(a))(a, f(a))(a,f(a)), mtan=lim⁡h→0f(a+h)−f(a)hm_{tan} = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}mtan​=limh→0​hf(a+h)−f(a)​, provided this limit exists. Derivative at a Point: f′(a)=lim⁡x→af(x)−f(a)x−af'(a) = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a}f′(a)=limx→a​x−af(x)−f(a)​ or f′(a)=lim⁡h→0f(a+h)−f(a)hf'(a) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}f′(a)=limh→0​hf(a+h)−f(a)​. If f′(a)f'(a)f′(a) exists, fff is differentiable at aaa. The Derivative as a Function: f′(x)=lim⁡h→0f(x+h)−f(x)hf'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}f′(x)=limh→0​hf(x+h)−f(x)​. If f′(x)f'(x)f′(x) exists, fff is differentiable at xxx. If fff is differentiable at every point of an open interval III, fff is differentiable on III.

documents.t: Constant Rule (Theorem 3.2): ddx(c)=0\frac{d}{dx}(c) = 0dxd​(c)=0. Power Rule (Theorem 3.3): ddx(xn)=nxn−1\frac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}dxd​(xn)=nxn−1 for nnn a nonnegative integer. Constant Multiple Rule (Theorem 3.4): ddx(cf(x))=cf′(x)\frac{d}{dx}(cf(x)) = cf'(x)dxd​(cf(x))=cf′(x). Sum Rule (Theorem 3.5): ddx(f(x)+g(x))=f′(x)+g′(x)\frac{d}{dx}(f(x)+g(x)) = f'(x)+g'(x)dxd​(f(x)+g(x))=f′(x)+g′(x). Derivative of exe^xex (Theorem 3.6): ddx(ex)=ex\frac{d}{dx}(e^x) = e^xdxd​(ex)=ex. Product Rule (Theorem 3.7): ddx(f(x)g(x))=f′(x)g(x)+f(x)g′(x)\frac{d}{dx}(f(x)g(x)) = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x)dxd​(f(x)g(x))=f′(x)g(x)+f(x)g′(x). Quotient Rule (Theorem 3.8): ddx(f(x)g(x))=g(x)f′(x)−f(x)g′(x)(g(x))2\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}\right) = \frac{g(x)f'(x) - f(x)g'(x)}{(g(x))^2}dxd​(g(x)f(x)​)=(g(x))2g(x)f′(x)−f(x)g′(x)​, provided g(x)≠0g(x) \neq 0g(x)=0. Power Rule (General Form) (Theorem 3.9): ddx(xn)=nxn−1\frac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}dxd​(xn)=nxn−1 for any real number nnn. Derivatives of Trigonometric Functions (Theorem 3.12): ddx(sin⁡x)=cos⁡x\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos xdxd​(sinx)=cosx ddx(cos⁡x)=−sin⁡x\frac{d}{dx}(\cos x) = -\sin xdxd​(cosx)=−sinx ddx(tan⁡x)=sec⁡2x\frac{d}{dx}(\tan x) = \sec^2 xdxd​(tanx)=sec2x ddx(cot⁡x)=−csc⁡2x\frac{d}{dx}(\cot x) = -\csc^2 xdxd​(cotx)=−csc2x ddx(sec⁡x)=sec⁡xtan⁡x\frac{d}{dx}(\sec x) = \sec x \tan xdxd​(secx)=secxtanx ddx(csc⁡x)=−csc⁡xcot⁡x\frac{d}{dx}(\csc x) = -\csc x \cot xdxd​(cscx)=−cscxcotx

documents.t: Theorem: If a function fff has a derivative at x=cx=cx=c, then fff is continuous at x=cx=cx=c. (The converse is not true; continuity does not imply differentiability, e.g., f(x)=∣x∣f(x)=|x|f(x)=∣x∣ at x=0x=0x=0).

If f(a)=f(b)f(a) = f(b)f(a)=f(b), then there is at least one number ccc in (a,b)(a,b)(a,b) at which f′(c)=0f'(c) = 0f′(c)=0.

documents.t: Continuity Definition: ∣f(x)−f(p)∣|f(x) - f(p)| ∣f(x)−f(p)∣<ϵ \epsilonϵ whenever ∣x−p∣|x - p| ∣x−p∣<δ \deltaδ Derivative at a Point: f′(a)=lim⁡h→0f(a+h)−f(a)hf'(a) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}f′(a)=limh→0​hf(a+h)−f(a)​ Derivative as a Function: f′(x)=lim⁡h→0f(x+h)−f(x)hf'(x) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}f′(x)=limh→0​hf(x+h)−f(x)​ Constant Rule: ddx(c)=0\frac{d}{dx}(c) = 0dxd​(c)=0 Power Rule: ddx(xn)=nxn−1\frac{d}{dx}(x^n) = nx^{n-1}dxd​(xn)=nxn−1 Constant Multiple Rule: ddx(cf(x))=cf′(x)\frac{d}{dx}(cf(x)) = cf'(x)dxd​(cf(x))=cf′(x) Sum Rule: ddx(f(x)+g(x))=f′(x)+g′(x)\frac{d}{dx}(f(x)+g(x)) = f'(x)+g'(x)dxd​(f(x)+g(x))=f′(x)+g′(x) Derivative of exe^xex: ddx(ex)=ex\frac{d}{dx}(e^x) = e^xdxd​(ex)=ex Product Rule: ddx(f(x)g(x))=f′(x)g(x)+f(x)g′(x)\frac{d}{dx}(f(x)g(x)) = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x)dxd​(f(x)g(x))=f′(x)g(x)+f(x)g′(x) Quotient Rule: ddx(f(x)g(x))=g(x)f′(x)−f(x)g′(x)(g(x))2\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{f(x)}{g(x)}\right) = \frac{g(x)f'(x) - f(x)g'(x)}{(g(x))^2}dxd​(g(x)f(x)​)=(g(x))2g(x)f′(x)−f(x)g′(x)​ Chain Rule: dydx=dydududx\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{du} \frac{du}{dx}dxdy​=dudy​dxdu​ or ddx(f(g(x)))=f′(g(x))⋅g′(x)\frac{d}{dx}(f(g(x))) = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)dxd​(f(g(x)))=f′(g(x))⋅g′(x) Velocity: v(t)=f′(t)v(t) = f'(t)v(t)=f′(t) Acceleration: a(t)=f′′(t)a(t) = f''(t)a(t)=f′′(t)

documents.t: This lecture covered the fundamental definitions of continuity (epsilon-delta) and differentiability (limit definitions), establishing the relationship where differentiability implies continuity. Key theorems such as the Intermediate Value Theorem (including Bolzano's Theorem) and Rolle's Theorem were introduced, providing existence guarantees for function values or derivatives under specific conditions. Essential differentiation rules for sums, products, quotients, constants, powers, and trigonometric functions were detailed, along with the powerful Chain Rule for composite functions and the concept of higher-order derivatives.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Single Variable Calculus/Lec_02_Continuity and Differentiability

documents.t: Definition: The Mean Value Theorem (MVT) If y=f(x)y = f(x)y=f(x) is continuous on a closed interval [a,b][a, b][a,b] and differentiable on the open interval (a,b)(a, b)(a,b), then there exists at least one point ccc in (a,b)(a, b)(a,b) such that: f′(c)=f(b)−f(a)b−af'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a}f′(c)=b−af(b)−f(a)​ This means the instantaneous rate of change at ccc is equal to the average rate of change over the interval [a,b][a, b][a,b]. Geometrically, there is a tangent line at ccc that is parallel to the secant line connecting (a,f(a))(a, f(a))(a,f(a)) and (b,f(b))(b, f(b))(b,f(b)). Remark on MVT Hypotheses: Continuity at the endpoints of the interval is essential for the Mean Value Theorem, while differentiability is only required for the interval's interior (a,b)(a, b)(a,b), not necessarily at the endpoints aaa and bbb.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Single Variable Calculus/Lec_03_Mean Value Theorem and Monotonic Functions

documents.t: Corollary 1: Functions with Zero Derivatives Are Constant If f′(x)=0f'(x) = 0f′(x)=0 at each point xxx of an open interval (a,b)(a, b)(a,b), then f(x)=Cf(x) = Cf(x)=C for all x∈(a,b)x \in (a, b)x∈(a,b), where CCC is a constant. Corollary 2: Functions with the Same Derivative Differ by a Constant If f′(x)=g′(x)f'(x) = g'(x)f′(x)=g′(x) at each point xxx in an open interval (a,b)(a, b)(a,b), then there exists a constant CCC such that f(x)=g(x)+Cf(x) = g(x) + Cf(x)=g(x)+C for all x∈(a,b)x \in (a, b)x∈(a,b). This implies that the difference function f−gf - gf−g is constant on (a,b)(a, b)(a,b).
documents.s: Corollaries of the Mean Value Theorem
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Single Variable Calculus/Lec_03_Mean Value Theorem and Monotonic Functions

documents.t: Definitions: Increasing, Decreasing, and Monotonic Functions Let fff be a function defined on an interval III, and let x1,x2x_1, x_2x1​,x2​ be any two points in III: Increasing Function: If f(x1)f(x_1) f(x1​)<f(x2) f(x_2)f(x2​) whenever x1x_1 x1​<x2 x_2x2​, then fff is said to be increasing on III. Decreasing Function: If f(x2)f(x_2) f(x2​)<f(x1) f(x_1)f(x1​) whenever x1x_1 x1​<x2 x_2x2​, then fff is said to be decreasing on III. Monotonic Function: A function that is either increasing or decreasing on an interval III is called monotonic on III.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Single Variable Calculus/Lec_03_Mean Value Theorem and Monotonic Functions

documents.t: Test for Local Extrema Suppose ccc is a critical point of a continuous function fff, and fff is differentiable at every point in some interval containing ccc (except possibly at ccc itself). As xxx moves across ccc from left to right: If f′(x)f'(x)f′(x) changes from negative to positive at ccc, then fff has a local minimum at ccc. If f′(x)f'(x)f′(x) changes from positive to negative at ccc, then fff has a local maximum at ccc. If f′(x)f'(x)f′(x) does not change sign at ccc (i.e., f′(x)f'(x)f′(x) is positive on both sides of ccc or negative on both sides), then fff has no local extremum at ccc.
documents.s: First Derivative Test for Local Extrema

documents.t: The Mean Value Theorem states that for a continuous and differentiable function on a closed interval, there exists at least one point where the instantaneous rate of change equals the overall average rate of change. Its corollaries demonstrate that functions with a zero derivative are constant, and functions with identical derivatives differ only by a constant. A function is defined as monotonic if it is either consistently increasing or consistently decreasing over an interval. The First Derivative Test uses the change in sign of the first derivative around critical points to precisely identify local maxima and minima.

documents.t: DEFINITION: Concave Up, Concave Down The graph of a differentiable function y=f(x)y = f(x)y=f(x) is: Concave Up on an open interval III if f′f'f′ is increasing on III. Concave Down on an open interval III if f′f'f′ is decreasing on III.

documents.t: THEOREM: Second Derivative Test for Concavity Let y=f(x)y = f(x)y=f(x) be twice-differentiable on an interval III. If f′′(x)f''(x) f′′(x)>0 00 on III, the graph of fff over III is concave up. If f′′(x)f''(x) f′′(x)<0 00 on III, the graph of fff over III is concave down.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Single Variable Calculus/Lec_04_Concavity, Linear Approximation, and L'Hôpital's Rule

documents.t: DEFINITION: Point of Inflection A point where the graph of a function has a tangent line and where the concavity changes is a point of inflection. A point of inflection may occur where f′′(x)=0f''(x) = 0f′′(x)=0 or where f′′(x)f''(x)f′′(x) does not exist, provided the concavity actually changes sign around that point.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Single Variable Calculus/Lec_04_Concavity, Linear Approximation, and L'Hôpital's Rule

documents.t: THEOREM: Second Derivative Test for Local Extrema Suppose f′′(x)f''(x)f′′(x) is continuous on an open interval containing ccc with f′(c)=0f'(c) = 0f′(c)=0. If f′′(c)f''(c) f′′(c)>0 00, then fff has a local minimum at ccc. If f′′(c)f''(c) f′′(c)<0 00, then fff has a local maximum at ccc. If f′′(c)=0f''(c) = 0f′′(c)=0, the test is inconclusive; fff may have a local maximum, a local minimum, or neither at ccc.
documents.s: Second Derivative Test for Local Extrema
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Single Variable Calculus/Lec_04_Concavity, Linear Approximation, and L'Hôpital's Rule

documents.t: DEFINITION: Linear Approximation to fff at aaa If fff is differentiable on an interval III containing aaa, the linear approximation to fff at aaa is the linear function: L(x)=f(a)+f′(a)(x−a)L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)L(x)=f(a)+f′(a)(x−a) Graphically, L(x)L(x)L(x) represents the tangent line to f(x)f(x)f(x) at x=ax=ax=a. If fff is concave up at (a,f(a))(a, f(a))(a,f(a)), L(x)L(x)L(x) underestimates f(x)f(x)f(x) near aaa. If fff is concave down at (a,f(a))(a, f(a))(a,f(a)), L(x)L(x)L(x) overestimates f(x)f(x)f(x) near aaa.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Single Variable Calculus/Lec_04_Concavity, Linear Approximation, and L'Hôpital's Rule

documents.t: THEOREM: L'Hôpital's Rule Suppose fff and ggg are differentiable on an open interval III containing aaa with g′(x)≠0g'(x) \neq 0g′(x)=0 on III when x≠ax \neq ax=a. If lim⁡x→af(x)=0\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = 0limx→a​f(x)=0 and lim⁡x→ag(x)=0\lim_{x \to a} g(x) = 0limx→a​g(x)=0, then: lim⁡x→af(x)g(x)=lim⁡x→af′(x)g′(x)\lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f'(x)}{g'(x)}limx→a​g(x)f(x)​=limx→a​g′(x)f′(x)​ This rule applies provided the limit on the right exists (or is ±∞\pm \infty±∞). It also applies when x→±∞x \to \pm \inftyx→±∞, x→a+x \to a^+x→a+, or x→a−x \to a^-x→a−.
documents.u: /classroom-to-gh-pages/MA 103 Calculus  of Single Variable and Linear Algebra/Single Variable Calculus/Lec_04_Concavity, Linear Approximation, and L'Hôpital's Rule

documents.t: THEOREM: L'Hôpital's Rule (∞/∞\infty/\infty∞/∞) Suppose fff and ggg are differentiable on an open interval III containing aaa with g′(x)≠0g'(x) \neq 0g′(x)=0 on III when x≠ax \neq ax=a. If lim⁡x→af(x)=±∞\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = \pm \inftylimx→a​f(x)=±∞ and lim⁡x→ag(x)=±∞\lim_{x \to a} g(x) = \pm \inftylimx→a​g(x)=±∞, then: lim⁡x→af(x)g(x)=lim⁡x→af′(x)g′(x)\lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f'(x)}{g'(x)}limx→a​g(x)f(x)​=limx→a​g′(x)f′(x)​ This rule applies provided the limit on the right exists (or is ±∞\pm \infty±∞). It also applies when x→±∞x \to \pm \inftyx→±∞, x→a+x \to a^+x→a+, or x→a−x \to a^-x→a−.

documents.t: THEOREM: Cauchy's Mean Value Theorem Suppose functions fff and ggg are continuous on [a,b][a, b][a,b] and differentiable throughout (a,b)(a, b)(a,b), and g′(x)≠0g'(x) \neq 0g′(x)=0 throughout (a,b)(a, b)(a,b). Then there exists a number ccc in (a,b)(a, b)(a,b) at which: f′(c)g′(c)=f(b)−f(a)g(b)−g(a)\frac{f'(c)}{g'(c)} = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{g(b) - g(a)}g′(c)f′(c)​=g(b)−g(a)f(b)−f(a)​

documents.t: THEOREM: Darboux's Theorem Let III be a closed interval, and f:I→Rf: I \to \mathbb{R}f:I→R be a differentiable function. Then f′f'f′ has an intermediate value property: if a,b∈Ia, b \in Ia,b∈I with aa a<b bb, then for every yyy between f′(a)f'(a)f′(a) and f′(b)f'(b)f′(b), there exists x∈[a,b]x \in [a, b]x∈[a,b] such that f′(x)=yf'(x) = yf′(x)=y.

documents.t: Concavity: f′′(x)f''(x) f′′(x)>0 ⟹ 0 \implies0⟹ Concave Up f′′(x)f''(x) f′′(x)<0 ⟹ 0 \implies0⟹ Concave Down Local Extrema (Second Derivative Test): If f′(c)=0f'(c) = 0f′(c)=0 and f′′(c)f''(c) f′′(c)>0 ⟹ 0 \implies0⟹ Local Minimum at ccc. If f′(c)=0f'(c) = 0f′(c)=0 and f′′(c)f''(c) f′′(c)<0 ⟹ 0 \implies0⟹ Local Maximum at ccc. Linear Approximation: L(x)=f(a)+f′(a)(x−a)L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)L(x)=f(a)+f′(a)(x−a) L'Hôpital's Rule: (for 0/00/00/0 or ∞/∞\infty/\infty∞/∞ forms) lim⁡x→af(x)g(x)=lim⁡x→af′(x)g′(x)\lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f'(x)}{g'(x)}limx→a​g(x)f(x)​=limx→a​g′(x)f′(x)​ Cauchy's Mean Value Theorem: f′(c)g′(c)=f(b)−f(a)g(b)−g(a)\frac{f'(c)}{g'(c)} = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{g(b) - g(a)}g′(c)f′(c)​=g(b)−g(a)f(b)−f(a)​
